mnemonics.title: Miller's 7±2 Rule
mnemonics.content: Remember: 'MAGICAL 7' - Most Adults Generally Can Imagine About 7 items Listed

mnemonics.title: Memory Types Remembering
mnemonics.content: S.W.L.P. - Sensory holds briefly, Working lasts pennies, Long-term persists forever

mnemonics.title: Serial Position Effect
mnemonics.content: P.R.I.M.A - Primary (first), Recency (last), Middle (ignored), Always place important at ends

acronyms.usage: The visual elements users interact with
Overall user interaction with the product
The active processing memory with ~7 item capacity

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a small tray that can hold only 5-7 objects. If you add more, things fall off. This is why we limit menu items.
Think of a necklace - individual beads (digits) are hard to carry, but grouped as a chain (chunks) are easy to manage.
Recognition is like finding your face in a photo (easy). Recall is describing your face to a sketch artist from memory (hard). UI should show faces not ask for descriptions.

analogies.analogy: Working memory is like a desk - small surface area. You can only keep a few papers out at once. Stack or file the rest to free up space.
Cognitive load is like a computer's RAM. Too many programs open = slow performance. Close unnecessary programs = smooth operation.
Forgetting is like ice melting. Without reinforcement (putting back in freezer), the memory 'melts' away quickly at first, then stabilizes.
Individual letters (U-N-I-X) are hard to remember. Chunks (UNIX) become one easy-to-remember item. Same with phone numbers and credit cards.
